comment_text.sn3.13:1.1: This Sutta presents what is probably the world’s first weight loss diet.
comment_text.sn3.13:1.2: A doṇa (“bucketful”) was a wooden trough to receive the filtered Soma (Rig Veda 4.32.17). In the Arthaśāstra it measures feed for horses (2.30.12) and elephants (2.15.50). One doṇa was the monthly allotment of grain for a servant (Manu 7.126). Olivelle estimates it at 5 liters or 9.6 kg (King, Governance, and Law, appendix 2, pg. 458). | The use of present tense (bhuñjati) here indicates that it is an ongoing situation, not just a single large meal (compare the aorist in the previous Sutta).
comment_text.sn3.13:3.3: For vedanā as “discomfort”, compare the stock reflection on eating (SN 35.120:4.3).
comment_text.sn3.13:4.1: This Vedic student attending the king is otherwise unknown.
comment_text.sn3.13:4.3: This passages well illustrates the meaning of pariyāpuṇāti as “memorize”.
comment_text.sn3.13:4.4: Niccabhikkhā at Ja 398:4.1 refers to the daily offering of food to a yakkha. Here and at MN 94:33.1 it refers to money.
comment_text.sn3.13:6.1: Nālikā was a cup made from a hollow bamboo. It was a modest meal for a king (SN 22.96:4.10, DN 17:2.15.10), sufficient for a medium-sized mendicant’s bowl (Bi NP 1:2.1.10). It does not appear in this sense in the Arthaśāstra, but the Indian Epigraphical Glossary says it is one-sixteenth of a doṇa and equivalent to a prastha. Olivelle estimates the prastha at around 600g, which is indeed one sixteenth of a doṇa. Bear in mind that rice constituted the bulk of the meal, and for monastics, it would often have been the only meal of the day.
comment_text.sn3.13:6.2: Interesting use of sallikhati = sallekha (“cut down” = “slimmed”), related to the idea of “effacing” writing as the stone wears away. For the Jains, sallekhanā was the practice of refusing all food at the end of life (Tattvārthasūtra 7.22; compare the Buddhist practice at MN 8:4.3).

root_text.sn3.13:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena rājā pasenadi kosalo doṇapākakuraṁ bhuñjati.
root_text.sn3.13:1.3: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo bhuttāvī mahassāsī yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi.
root_text.sn3.13:2.1: Atha kho bhagavā rājānaṁ pasenadiṁ kosalaṁ bhuttāviṁ mahassāsiṁ viditvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn3.13:4.1: Tena kho pana samayena sudassano māṇavo rañño pasenadissa kosalassa piṭṭhito ṭhito hoti.
root_text.sn3.13:4.2: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo sudassanaṁ māṇavaṁ āmantesi:
root_text.sn3.13:4.3: “ehi tvaṁ, tāta sudassana, bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ pariyāpuṇitvā mama bhattābhihāre bhattābhihāre bhāsa.
root_text.sn3.13:4.4: Ahañca te devasikaṁ kahāpaṇasataṁ kahāpaṇasataṁ niccaṁ bhikkhaṁ pavattayissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn3.13:4.5: “Evaṁ, devā”ti kho sudassano māṇavo rañño pasenadissa kosalassa paṭissutvā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ pariyāpuṇitvā rañño pasenadissa kosalassa bhattābhihāre sudaṁ bhāsati:
root_text.sn3.13:6.1: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo anupubbena nāḷikodanaparamatāya saṇṭhāsi.
root_text.sn3.13:6.2: Atha kho rājā pasenadi kosalo aparena samayena susallikhitagatto pāṇinā gattāni anumajjanto tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ udānaṁ udānesi:
root_text.sn3.13:6.3: “ubhayena vata maṁ so bhagavā atthena anukampi—

translation_text.sn3.13:1.2: Now at that time King Pasenadi of Kosala used to eat boiled rice by the bucketful.
translation_text.sn3.13:1.3: Then after eating King Pasenadi of Kosala went up to the Buddha, huffing and puffing. He bowed and sat down to one side.
translation_text.sn3.13:2.1: Then, knowing that King Pasenadi was huffing and puffing after eating, on that occasion the Buddha spoke this verse:
translation_text.sn3.13:3.4: and he ages slowly, taking care of his life.”
translation_text.sn3.13:4.1: Now at that time the student Sudassana was standing behind the king.
translation_text.sn3.13:4.3: “Please, dear Sudassana, memorize this verse in the Buddha’s presence and recite it to me whenever I am served a meal.
translation_text.sn3.13:4.4: I’ll set up a regular daily allowance of a hundred dollars for you.”
translation_text.sn3.13:4.5: “Yes, Your Majesty,” replied Sudassana. He memorized that verse in the Buddha’s presence, and then whenever the king was served a meal he would repeat it:
translation_text.sn3.13:5.4: and he ages slowly, taking care of his life.”
translation_text.sn3.13:6.1: Then the king gradually got used to having at most a cup of rice.
translation_text.sn3.13:6.2: After some time King Pasenadi’s body slimmed right down. Stroking his limbs with his hands, at that time he expressed this heartfelt sentiment:
translation_text.sn3.13:6.3: “In both ways the Buddha has sympathy for me:
translation_text.sn3.13:6.4: in the good of this life and the good of the next.”

variant_text.sn3.13:1.2: doṇapākakuraṁ → doṇapākaṁ sudaṁ (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed); doṇapākasudaṁ (bj, cck); doṇapākasudhaṁ (sya1ed, sya2ed)
variant_text.sn3.13:4.3: bhattābhihāre → idaṁ padaṁ bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed potthakesu
variant_text.sn3.13:4.4: kahāpaṇasataṁ → idaṁ padaṁ bj, sya-all, km potthakesu
